title: Is it worth switching energy supplier in 2026?
description: Switching energy supplier in 2026 typically saves £45 to £125 a year on a standard tariff, and far more on the right EV or time-of-use deal. Here is the maths.
blurb: What switching actually saves in 2026, who gains the most, and a five-minute method to check your own deal.
lede: Ten years ago the answer was easy: switch every year, pocket £200 to £300, repeat. The price cap, the 2021 to 2023 energy crisis and a thinner field of suppliers changed that, and plenty of people quietly stopped bothering. That is a mistake in 2026, but for a different reason than the old one. The headline saving from hopping between standard tariffs is now modest. The real money sits in matching the type of tariff to the way your household uses energy, and for some homes that gap is worth several hundred pounds a year. This guide puts actual numbers on both.
short_answer: Yes, but the easy money is smaller than it used to be. Against the July to September 2026 price cap of 26.11p per kWh for electricity and 7.33p for gas, the better fixed deals undercut cap unit rates by roughly 3 to 8 per cent, worth about £45 to £125 a year for a typical dual-fuel home. The much bigger wins come from tariff type: an EV owner moving charging onto a 7p off-peak rate can save £500 or more a year compared with charging at the standard rate.

sections.html:
The switching market of the 2010s was built on a wide gap between expensive standard variable tariffs and cheap fixed deals from challenger suppliers. Dozens of those challengers went bust in 2021 when wholesale prices exploded, and for almost two years there was nothing worth switching to at all. The market has since reopened, but the shape of it has changed.

Two things now anchor every price you are offered. The first is Ofgem's price cap, which limits what suppliers can charge on default tariffs and is reset every three months. For July to September 2026 it works out at 26.11p per kWh for electricity and 7.33p per kWh for gas, with daily standing charges of 57.19p and 29.04p on the GB direct-debit average. Our guide to how the price cap works covers the mechanics.

The second anchor is the wholesale market, which is calmer than it was but still nervy. Suppliers price their fixed deals off it, and in mid 2026 the competitive fixes sit a few per cent below the cap rather than a long way under it. The cap protects people who never switch, but it also acts as a public benchmark that suppliers price just beneath. The result is a market where everyone clusters within about 10 per cent of the same number on standard tariffs, while the genuinely cheap electricity has moved into off-peak windows on time-of-use tariffs.

So the honest answer to the headline question splits in two: a small but real saving for almost everyone, and a large saving for households whose usage pattern fits a specialist tariff.

sections.h2: What a typical household saves by switching
sections.html:
Take Ofgem's typical dual-fuel household: 2,700 kWh of electricity and 11,500 kWh of gas a year, paying by direct debit. At July to September 2026 cap rates that household pays about £1,548 a year in unit charges plus £315 in standing charges, around £1,863 in total.

Standing charges on fixed deals usually sit close to the capped level, so the saving from a switch comes almost entirely from the unit rates. Here is what different levels of discount are worth on that typical usage.

ScenarioAnnual cost (typical use)Saving vs the cap
Stay on the price cap (standard variable)£1,863£0
Fix with unit rates 3% below cap£1,817£46
Fix with unit rates 5% below cap£1,786£77
Fix with unit rates 8% below cap£1,739£124

At 26.11p per kWh electricity and 7.33p per kWh gas, with standing charges of 57.19p and 29.04p per day. Ofgem price cap, July to September 2026, GB direct-debit average. Typical use 2,700 kWh electricity and 11,500 kWh gas.

£46 to £124 a year for a form-filling job that takes fifteen minutes is still a decent hourly rate, and a fix also buys protection if the cap rises during your contract. But it explains why switching no longer feels exciting: on a like-for-like tariff, the gap between the best and the default has narrowed to tens of pounds, not hundreds. Whether to take a fix at all is a separate judgement, covered in our guide to fixed versus variable tariffs.

sections.h2: Who saves a lot more than £100
sections.html:
The averages hide the households for whom switching is the single biggest energy saving available. If any of these describe you, the question is not whether to switch but how soon.

EV drivers charging at standard rates

This is the largest gap in the whole market. A car doing 10,000 miles a year at 3.5 miles per kWh needs about 2,857 kWh. Charged at the capped rate of 26.11p that costs roughly £746 a year. On an EV tariff with a 7p off-peak window it costs about £200, and the cheapest smart-charging tariffs in mid 2026 price overnight units near 5.5p. That is a saving of £500 to £590 a year from one switch, before you touch anything else in the house. See EV electricity tariffs explained for how the off-peak windows work.

Storage heater and electric-only homes

If you heat with storage heaters or have high overnight use, an Economy 7 tariff charges night units well below the single rate. Electric-only homes use far more kWh than average, so a few pence per unit matters more to them than to anyone else. The same logic applies in reverse: if you are stuck on Economy 7 but use most of your power in the daytime, switching away can save money too.

People who have never switched, or whose fix has ended

When a fixed deal ends you roll onto your supplier's standard variable tariff at cap rates. If you fixed cheaply in the past, your bill jumps. Anyone in this position should run the comparison the moment the renewal letter arrives, and our guide to what happens when your fixed deal ends walks through the timing.

Prepayment and standard-credit payers

Paying on receipt of a quarterly bill costs more than direct debit on most tariffs, and moving from prepayment to direct debit where your supplier and credit history allow it usually cuts the unit price as well. The differences are smaller than they once were but still real money on a high bill.

sections.h2: How to compare deals properly in five minutes
sections.html:
Comparison sites do most of this for you, but they only get it right if you feed them your real consumption, and they do not always surface the tariffs that suit unusual usage patterns. The manual method is quick and keeps you honest.

  • Get your annual kWh for each fuel. It is on your bill or annual summary, or in your supplier's app. Do not guess from the monthly direct debit, which is a smoothed payment, not your usage. Our guide to understanding your energy bill shows where the figure hides.
  • Price each tariff with one sum per fuel. Annual kWh multiplied by the unit rate, plus 365 times the daily standing charge. Do it for your current tariff and the offer, then compare totals.
  • Check the standing charge trade-off. Some deals pair a low unit rate with a higher standing charge or the reverse. Low users (small flats, second homes) do better with a low standing charge; high users should chase the unit rate.
  • Check exit fees and length. Typical fees are £25 to £75 per fuel on fixed deals. A 12-month fix with no exit fee is worth a few pounds more than a locked-in one.
  • Check the smart meter requirement. Most time-of-use and EV tariffs need a working smart meter sending half-hourly readings. If you do not have one, the supplier will usually fit one when you join; our guide to smart meters covers what is involved.

Run the numbers per fuel, not just on the combined total. It is sometimes cheaper to take electricity and gas from different suppliers, though the gap is usually small; see dual fuel versus separate suppliers.

A switch changes the price of each unit. It does nothing about how many units you use, and for most homes usage is where the bigger money sits. A household burning 15,000 kWh of gas through poor insulation saves more from draught-proofing and heating controls than from any tariff on the market.

Standing charges are the other limit. They are broadly similar across suppliers because they recover network and policy costs that vary by region, not by supplier, so switching rarely cuts them by much. If yours look high, that is usually your region rather than your supplier; standing charges explained covers why, and why a zero-standing-charge tariff only suits very low users.

Finally, price is not the only variable. Suppliers differ widely on billing accuracy and complaint handling, and Citizens Advice publishes quarterly customer service ratings that are worth thirty seconds of your time before you commit. A £40 saving is poor compensation for a year of wrong bills. If you are in debt to your current supplier, you can normally still switch if the debt is under 28 days old or, on prepayment, under £500 per fuel, but check the current rules on the Ofgem website as they do change.

Switching is worth it in 2026, with realistic expectations. On a like-for-like standard tariff, a typical home saves £45 to £125 a year against the July to September cap, plus whatever protection a fix gives against the next quarterly reset. That is worth the fifteen minutes, every time your deal ends.

The households that should treat switching as urgent are the ones whose usage no longer matches their tariff: EV drivers charging at 26.11p when 7p windows exist, electric-heated homes on single-rate tariffs, and anyone who rolled silently onto a standard variable tariff months ago. For them the gap is £200 to £600 a year, which puts switching ahead of almost any other energy action short of insulation. Start with our hub on switching suppliers for the step-by-step process, and re-run the comparison whenever the cap changes or your fix ends.

faq.q: How much does switching energy supplier save in 2026?
faq.a: On a standard tariff, the better fixed deals undercut the July to September 2026 price cap unit rates by roughly 3 to 8 per cent, worth about £45 to £125 a year for a typical dual-fuel home. Specialist tariffs save far more for the right household: an EV driver moving charging from 26.11p to a 7p off-peak rate saves around £500 a year on 10,000 miles.

faq.q: Does switching supplier interrupt my gas or electricity?
faq.a: No. The energy arriving at your home is identical whoever bills you, and nothing physical changes. The new supplier handles the whole process, it usually completes within about five working days, and you simply give an opening meter reading. You also have a 14-day cooling-off period after agreeing the deal.

faq.q: Can I switch if I owe money to my current supplier?
faq.a: Usually yes if the debt is less than 28 days old, since it just gets added to your final bill. Older debt normally has to be repaid first, except on prepayment meters where you can transfer debt up to £500 per fuel to a new supplier under the Debt Assignment Protocol. Check the current rules on the Ofgem website as they change.

faq.q: Do I need a smart meter to switch energy supplier?
faq.a: Not for ordinary fixed or variable tariffs. You do need a working smart meter for most time-of-use and EV tariffs, because they bill against half-hourly readings. Suppliers will normally fit one for free when you join such a tariff.

faq.q: Is it better to fix my energy price or stay on the price cap in 2026?
faq.a: Fixing is cheaper if the deal undercuts the cap and the cap then rises during your contract; the cap is cheaper if it falls below your fixed price. In mid 2026 competitive fixes sit a few per cent below the July cap, so fixing mainly buys certainty plus a modest saving. Compare the actual unit rates and standing charges against the cap figures before deciding.
